--- SOURCE 1: Solar fuel - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel

SUMMARY:
A solar fuel is a synthetic fuel produced using solar energy, through photochemical (i.e. photonactivation of certain chemical reactions), photobiological (i.e., artificial photosynthesis), electrochemical (i.e. using solar electricity to drive an endogenic reaction such as hydroelectrolysis),[1][2][3][4] or thermochemical methods (i.e., through the use of solar heat supplied by concentrated solar thermal energy to drive a chemical reaction).[5][6]Sunlight is the primary energy source, with its radiant energy being transduced to chemical energy stored in bonds, typically by reducingprotons to hydrogen, or carbon dioxide to organic compounds. In a November 2021 publication in Nature, Aldo Steinfeld of Swiss technological university ETH Zurich reported an artificial photosynthesis where carbon dioxide and water vapour absorbed from the air are passed over a cerium oxide catalyst heated by concentrated solar power to produce hydrogen and carbon monoxide, transformed through the Fischer-Tropsch process into complex hydrocarbons forming methanol, a liquid fuel.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Synthetic chemical fuel produced from solar energy

A **solar fuel** is a [synthetic fuel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Synthetic_fuel "Synthetic fuel") produced using [solar energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy "Solar energy"), through photochemical (i.e. [photon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photon "Photon")[activation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Activation "Activation") of certain [chemical reactions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction")), photobiological (i.e., [artificial photosynthesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_photosynthesis "Artificial photosynthesis")), [electrochemical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrochemical "Electrochemical") (i.e. using [solar electricity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_electricity "Solar electricity") to drive an [endogenic reaction](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Endogenic_reaction&action=edit&redlink=1 "Endogenic reaction (page does not exist)") such as [hydroelectrolysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrolysis_of_water "Electrolysis of water")),[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Sunshine_to_Petrol-1)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-SunShot-2)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-NYT41013-3)[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-4) or [thermochemical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermochemical "Thermochemical") methods (i.e., through the use of solar heat supplied by concentrated solar thermal energy to drive a chemical reaction).[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-5)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-6)[Sunlight](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunlight "Sunlight") is the primary [energy source](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy_source "Energy source"), with its [radiant energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radiant_energy "Radiant energy") being [transduced](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transduction_(biophysics) "Transduction (biophysics)") to [chemical energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_energy "Chemical energy") stored in [bonds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_bond "Chemical bond"), typically by [reducing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reduction_(chemistry) "Reduction (chemistry)")[protons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protons "Protons") to [hydrogen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen"), or [carbon dioxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") to [organic compounds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organic_compounds "Organic compounds").

A solar fuel can be produced and stored for later use, when sunlight is not available, making it an alternative to [fossil fuels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fossil_fuels "Fossil fuels") and batteries. Examples of such fuels are hydrogen, ammonia, and hydrazine. Diverse [photocatalysts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photocatalysts "Photocatalysts") are being developed to carry these reactions in a sustainable, [environmentally friendly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environmentally_friendly "Environmentally friendly") way.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Styring-7)

Overview
--------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Solar_fuel&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

The world's dependence on the declining reserves of fossil fuels poses not only [environmental problems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environmental_problems "Environmental problems") but also [geopolitical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geopolitical "Geopolitical") ones.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Hammarstrom-8) Solar fuels, in particular hydrogen, are viewed as an alternative source of energy for replacing fossil fuels especially where storage is essential. [Electricity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electricity "Electricity") can be produced directly from sunlight through [photovoltaics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photovoltaics "Photovoltaics"), but this form of energy is rather inefficient to store compared to hydrogen.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Styring-7) A solar fuel can be produced when and where sunlight is available, and stored and transported for later usage. This makes it much more convenient, because it can be used in situations where direct sunlight is not available.

The most widely researched solar fuels are hydrogen, because the only product of using this fuel is water, and products of [photochemical carbon dioxide reduction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photochemical_carbon_dioxide_reduction "Photochemical carbon dioxide reduction"), which are more conventional fuels like methane and propane. Upcoming research also involves ammonia and related substances (i.e. hydrazine). These can address the challenges that come with hydrogen, by being a more compact and safer way of storing hydrogen. Direct ammonia fuel cells are also being researched.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-:0-9)

Solar fuels can be produced via direct or indirect processes. Direct processes harness the energy in sunlight to produce a fuel without intermediary energy conversions. Solar thermochemistry uses the heat of the sun directly to heat a receiver adjacent to the solar reactor where the thermochemical process is performed. In contrast, indirect processes have solar energy converted to another form of energy first (such as [biomass](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biomass "Biomass") or electricity) that can then be used to produce a fuel. Indirect processes have been easier to implement but have the disadvantage of being less efficient than the direct method. Therefore, direct methods should be considered more interesting than their less efficient counterparts. New research therefore focusses more on this direct conversion, but also in fuels that can be used immediately to balance the power grid.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Styring-7)

Hydrogen production
-------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Solar_fuel&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Hydrogen production")]

See also: [Hydrogen production](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen_production "Hydrogen production")

### Photoelectrochemical

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Solar_fuel&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Photoelectrochemical")]

See also: [Photoelectrolysis of water](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photoelectrolysis_of_water "Photoelectrolysis of water")

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Photo_Electric_Cell_Evolving_Hydrogen_and_Oxygen.jpg/250px-Photo_Electric_Cell_Evolving_Hydrogen_and_Oxygen.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Photo_Electric_Cell_Evolving_Hydrogen_and_Oxygen.jpg)

A sample of a photoelectric cell in a lab environment. Catalysts are added to the cell, which is submerged in water and illuminated by simulated sunlight. The bubbles seen are oxygen (forming on the front of the cell) and hydrogen (forming on the back of the cell).

In a solar **photoelectrochemical** process, hydrogen can be produced by [electrolysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrolysis "Electrolysis"). To use sunlight in this process, a [photoelectrochemical cell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photoelectrochemical_cell "Photoelectrochemical cell") can be used, where one [photosensitized](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photosensitizer "Photosensitizer")[electrode](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrode "Electrode") converts light into an electric current that is then used for [water splitting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_splitting "Water splitting"). One such type of cell is the [dye-sensitized solar cell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dye-sensitized_solar_cell "Dye-sensitized solar cell").[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Kalyanasundaram-10) This is an indirect process, since it produces electricity that then is used to form hydrogen. Another indirect process using sunlight is conversion of biomass to [biofuel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel") using [photosynthetic organisms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phototroph "Phototroph"); however, most of the energy harvested by [photosynthesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photosynthesis "Photosynthesis") is used in life-sustaining processes and therefore lost for energy use.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Styring-7)

A [semiconductor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor") can also be used as the photosensitizer. When a semiconductor is hit by a photon with an energy higher than the [bandgap](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Band_gap "Band gap"), an electron is excited to the conduction band and a hole is created in the valence band. Due to band bending, the electrons and holes move to the surface, where these charges are used to split the water molecules. Many different materials have been tested, but none so far have shown the requirements for practical application.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-11)

### Photochemical

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Solar_fuel&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Photochemical")]

See also: [Photocatalytic water splitting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photocatalytic_water_splitting "Photocatalytic water splitting")

In a **photochemical** process, the sunlight is directly used to split water into hydrogen and oxygen. Because the absorption spectrum of water does not overlap with the emission spectrum of the sun, direct dissociation of water cannot take place; a photosensitizer needs to be used. Several such catalysts have been developed as [proof of concept](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proof_of_concept "Proof of concept"), but not yet scaled up for commercial use; nevertheless, their relative simplicity gives the advantage of potential lower cost and increased [energy conversion efficiency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy_conversion_efficiency "Energy conversion efficiency").[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Styring-7)[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Andreiadis-12) One such proof of concept is the "artificial leaf" developed by [Nocera](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_G._Nocera "Daniel G. Nocera") and coworkers: a combination of [metal oxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metal_oxide "Metal oxide")-based catalysts and a [semiconductor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor")[solar cell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_cell "Solar cell") produces hydrogen upon illumination, with [oxygen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") as the only byproduct.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Reece-13)

### Photobiological

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Solar_fuel&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Photobiological")]

In a **photobiological** process, the hydrogen is produced using photosynthetic microorganisms (green [microalgae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microalgae "Microalgae") and [cyanobacteria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyanobacteria "Cyanobacteria")) in [photobioreactors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photobioreactors "Photobioreactors"). Some of these organisms produce hydrogen upon switching [culture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Growth_medium "Growth medium") conditions; for example, _[Chlamydomonas reinhardtii](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chlamydomonas\_reinhardtii "Chlamydomonas reinhardtii")_ produces hydrogen [anaerobically](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Facultative_anaerobic_organism "Facultative anaerobic organism") under [sulfur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur") deprivation, that is, when cells are moved from one growth medium to another that does not contain sulfur, and are grown without access to atmospheric oxygen.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Kosourov-14) Another approach was to abolish activity of the hydrogen-oxidizing (uptake) [hydrogenase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogenase "Hydrogenase")[enzyme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") in the [diazotrophic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diazotroph "Diazotroph") cyanobacterium _[Nostoc punctiforme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nostoc\_punctiforme "Nostoc punctiforme")_, so that it would not consume hydrogen that is naturally produced by the [nitrogenase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nitrogenase "Nitrogenase") enzyme in [nitrogen-fixing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nitrogen-fixing "Nitrogen-fixing") conditions.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Lindberg-15) This _N. punctiforme_[mutant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mutant "Mutant") could then produce hydrogen when illuminated with [visible light](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visible_light "Visible light").

Another mutant [Cyanobacteria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyanobacteria "Cyanobacteria"), [Synechocystis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Synechocystis "Synechocystis"), is using genes of the bacteria Rubrivivax gelatinosus CBS to produce hydrogen. The CBS bacteria produce hydrogen through the oxidation of carbon monoxide. Researchers are working to implement these genes into the Synechocystis. If these genes can be applied, it will take some effort to overcome the problems of oxygen inhibition in the production of hydrogen, but it is estimated that this process can potentially yield as much as 10% solar energy capture. This makes photobiological research a very exciting and promising branch of the hydrogen production explorations. Still the problems of overcoming the short-term nature of algal hydrogen production are many and research is in the early stages. However, this research provides a viable way to industrialize these renewable and environmental friendly processes.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-16)

### Thermochemical

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Solar_fuel&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Thermochemical")]

See also: [Thermochemical cycle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermochemical_cycle "Thermochemical cycle")

In the solar **thermochemical**[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-17) process, water is split into hydrogen and oxygen using direct solar heat, rather than electricity, inside a high temperature solar reactor[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-18) which receives highly concentrated solar flux from a solar field of heliostats that focus the highly concentrated sunlight into the reactor.

The two most promising routes are the two step [cerium oxide cycle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cerium(IV)_oxide%E2%80%93cerium(III)_oxide_cycle "Cerium(IV) oxide–cerium(III) oxide cycle") and the [copper chlorine hybrid cycle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copper%E2%80%93chlorine_cycle "Copper–chlorine cycle"). For the cerium oxide cycle the first step is to strip the CeO 3 into Ce 2 O 3 at more than 1400°C. After the thermal reduction step to reduce the metal oxide, hydrogen is then produced through hydrolysis at around 800°C.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-:1-19)[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-20) The copper chloride cycle requires a lower temperature (~500°C), which makes this process more efficient, but the cycle contains more steps and is also more complex than the cerium oxide cycle.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-:1-19)

Because hydrogen manufacture requires continuous performance, the solar thermochemical process includes [thermal energy storage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_energy_storage "Thermal energy storage").[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-21) Another thermochemical method uses solar reforming of methane, a process that replicates traditional [fossil fuel reforming process](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steam_reforming "Steam reforming") but substitutes solar heat.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-22)

In a November 2021 publication in [Nature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)"), Aldo Steinfeld of Swiss technological university [ETH Zurich](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ETH_Zurich "ETH Zurich") reported an [artificial photosynthesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_photosynthesis "Artificial photosynthesis") where [carbon dioxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") and water vapour absorbed from the air are passed over a cerium oxide catalyst heated by [concentrated solar power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Concentrated_solar_power "Concentrated solar power") to produce hydrogen and carbon monoxide, transformed through the [Fischer-Tropsch process](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fischer-Tropsch_process "Fischer-Tropsch process") into complex hydrocarbons forming [methanol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Methanol "Methanol"), a [liquid fuel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liquid_fuel "Liquid fuel"). [Scaling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") could produce the 414 billion L (414 million m 3) of [aviation fuel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aviation_fuel "Aviation fuel") used in 2019 with a surface of 45,000 km 2 (17,000 sq mi): 0.5% of the [Sahara Desert](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sahara_Desert "Sahara Desert").[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-23)[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-24)[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-25) One author, Philipp Furler, leads specialist [Synhelion](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Synhelion&action=edit&redlink=1 "Synhelion (page does not exist)"), which in 2022 was building a solar fuel production facility at [Jülich](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J%C3%BClich "Jülich"), west of [Cologne](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), before another one in Spain.[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Flight1mar2022-26)[Swiss Airlines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Swiss_Airlines "Swiss Airlines"), part of the [Lufthansa Group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lufthansa_Group "Lufthansa Group"), should become its first customer in 2023.[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Flight1mar2022-26)

Carbon dioxide reduction
------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Solar_fuel&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Carbon dioxide reduction")]

See also: [Electrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrochemical_reduction_of_carbon_dioxide "Electrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide"), [Photoelectrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photoelectrochemical_reduction_of_carbon_dioxide "Photoelectrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide"), [Photochemical reduction of carbon dioxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photochemical_reduction_of_carbon_dioxide "Photochemical reduction of carbon dioxide"), and [Biofuel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel")

[Carbon dioxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") (CO 2) can be reduced to [carbon monoxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide") (CO) and other more reduced compounds, such as [methane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Methane "Methane"), using the appropriate photocatalysts. One early example was the use of [Tris(bipyridine)ruthenium(II) chloride](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tris(bipyridine)ruthenium(II)_chloride "Tris(bipyridine)ruthenium(II) chloride") (Ru(bipy)3 Cl 2) and [cobalt chloride](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cobalt_chloride "Cobalt chloride") (CoCl 2) for CO 2 reduction to CO.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-Lehn-27) In recent years many new catalysts have been found to reduce CO 2 into CO, after which the CO could be used to make hydrocarbons using for example the [Fischer-Tropsch process](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fischer%E2%80%93Tropsch_process "Fischer–Tropsch process"). The most promising system for the solar-powered reduction of CO 2 is the combination of a photovoltaic cell with an electrochemical cell (PV+EC).[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-28)[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-:2-29) Using solar-driven processes, CO 2 can also be converted to other products such as formate and alcohols.[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-30)[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-31)

For the photovoltaic cell the highly efficient GaInP/GaAs/Ge [solar cell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_cell "Solar cell") has been used, but many other series-connected and/or tandem (multi-junction) PV architectures can be employed to deliver the required voltage and current density to drive the CO 2 reduction reactions and provide reasonable product outflow.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-:4-32) The solar cells/panels can be placed in direct contact with the electrolyzer(s), which can bring advantages in terms of system compactness and thermal management of both technologies,[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-:4-32) or separately for instance by placing the PV outdoors exposed to sunlight and the EC systems protected indoors.[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-33)

The currently best performing electrochemical cell is the gas diffusion electrode (GED) flow cell. In which the CO 2 reacts on Ag nanoparticles to produce CO. Solar to CO efficiencies of up to 19% have been reached, with minimal loss in activity after 20h.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel#cite_note-:2-29)

CO can also be produced without a catalyst using microwave [plasma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasma_(physics) "Plasma (physics)") driven dissociation of CO 2. This process is relatively efficient, with an electricity to CO efficiency of up to 50%, but with low conversion around 10%. These low conversions are not ideal, because CO and CO 2 are hard to separate at large scale in a efficie

Breadcrumb
----------

1.  [Office of Science](https://www.energy.gov/science)
2.  [DOE Explains...Solar Fuels](https://www.energy.gov/science/doe-explainssolar-fuels)

DOE Explains...Solar Fuels
==========================

![Image 5: Energy production via solar fuels could recreate the starting chemicals, forming a closed cycle that minimizes unwanted by-products.](https://www.energy.gov/sites/default/files/styles/full_article_width/public/2024-07/doe-explains-solar-fuels.jpg?itok=DMRktx_q)

Energy production via solar fuels could recreate the starting chemicals, forming a closed cycle that minimizes unwanted by-products.

Image courtesy of the Department of Energy Office of Science

Solar fuels are fuels made from common substances like water and carbon dioxide using the energy of sunlight. There is vast energy in sunlight striking the earth, but it is dispersed and varies over time, making it challenging to harness sunlight for practical use. We have successfully tapped solar energy to make electricity but aren’t yet able to efficiently make liquid fuels from it. Solar fuels could be an abundant supply of sustainable, storable, and portable energy.

Solar fuels could diversify our fuel supply and increase the sustainability of our overall energy system. They could also use existing fuel infrastructure for a huge range of applications. These fuels could be stored for hours, days, months, or longer. Solar fuels could be transported anywhere, making them a valuable and flexible resource for a more reliable electric power grid.

One possible approach to producing solar fuels is “artificial photosynthesis.” This approach could work similarly to natural photosynthesis in plants by using only water, carbon dioxide, and sunlight to generate fuel.

Options for solar fuels could include processes to make hydrogen as a fuel by using solar energy to split water, or to produce alcohols such as ethanol and methanol by using solar energy to reduce carbon dioxide with hydrogen, or to create less-conventional fuels such as ammonia and hydrazine by using solar energy to reduce nitrogen with hydrogen.

DOE Office of Science: Contributions to Solar Fuels
---------------------------------------------------

The DOE Office of Science, Office of Basic Energy Sciences (BES) has been studying natural and artificial photosynthesis for more than 40 years. BES is one of the largest supporters of fundamental research into solar fuels. It funds individual investigators, small research teams, and larger multi-investigator, multi-disciplinary centers. The [Fuels from Sunlight Energy Innovation Hub](https://solarfuelshub.org/) is the largest effort. The hub focuses on developing the foundations for an effective system to convert solar energy to chemical fuels. Thanks to long-term support, scientists are making considerable progress toward understanding fundamental principles and components for making solar fuels. However, we still face many unanswered questions to make these fuels a commercial reality—how to control chemical reaction pathways to selectively and efficiently produce the specific fuels we want, how to effectively predict and design components with long lifetimes and desired activity, whether unique interactions between light and matter can be used for efficient and selective fuel generation, and how to identify and control individual processes that affect the function of integrated components for solar fuels production.

Solar Fuels Facts
-----------------

*   The amount of solar energy striking the earth's surface in **one hour** (about 170 petawatt hours of energy) is enough to support the world's energy consumption for an **entire year** (about 160 petawatt hours of energy).
*   Melvin Calvin, supported by DOE (through its predecessor, the Atomic Energy Commission), received the 1961 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for uncovering the mechanism nature uses during photosynthesis to convert carbon dioxide into energy-rich chemicals.

--- SOURCE 3: Renewable fuels for sustainable mobility - Synhelion ---
URL: https://synhelion.com/our-products

SUMMARY:
Solar fuels are synthetic drop-in fuels produced from solar energy. They are the most economically viable, efficient, scalable, and environmentally friendly

FULL CONTENT:
![Synhelion]()
![Synhelion]()

# The clean alternative to fossil fuels

# Sustainable fuels made of solar energy

Solar fuels are synthetic drop-in fuels produced from solar energy. They are the most economically viable, efficient, scalable, and environmentally friendly solution for clean, long-distance transportation. A cutting-edge technology that offers a sustainable alternative to fossil fuels.

![Three solar fuel barrels of Synhelion at plant DAWN in Jülich, Germany.](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/e25c664b18-1750836421/synhelion_solar-fuel-barrels.jpg)

# Our products

Synhelion produces solar fuels to defossilize the transportation sector. We produce solar jet fuel, diesel, and gasoline. Solar fuels are synthetically produced fuels designed to mimic the chemical and physical properties of fossil fuels. They are drop-in compatible with today’s engines and fuel infrastructure. This means that they can be blended with fossil-derived fuels or used in their pure form in conventional aircraft, ships, trucks, and cars.

## Sustainable Aviation Fuel

![An airplane flies across the blue sky.](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/f036239a63-1753791906/airplane.png)

[Learn more](https://synhelion.com/our-products/sustainable-aviation-fuel)

## Solar diesel

![](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/733ad6e91f-1753790981/ship_16-9.jpg)

[Learn more](https://synhelion.com/our-products/solar-diesel)

## Solar gasoline

![Several cars driving on a highway.](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/a88b4268ef-1741601583/automotive.jpg)

[Learn more](https://synhelion.com/our-products/solar-gasoline)

# Why solar fuels?

![](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/f209d97127-1741344927/icon-gas-station.svg)

**Compatible**

Solar fuels have the same chemical structure as fossil fuels. They are fully compatible with the global fuel infrastructure. There is no need to adapt or refit combustion engines, refineries, fuel distribution, or filling stations.

![](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/66dde578d2-1741344928/icon-affordable.svg)

**Affordable**

Solar fuels are the most economical way for producing sustainable synthetic fuels. It is our goal to reach production costs below EUR 1 per liter.

![](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/b283f1f0cf-1741344927/icon-swiss-army-knive.svg)

**Versatile**

Jet fuel for planes, gasoline for cars, marine fuel for ships, and diesel for trucks – Synhelion technology yields any type of fuel. Solar fuels can replace fossil fuels in all application areas.

![](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/c083a90e4f-1741344928/icon-rocket.svg)

**Scalable**

We use the power of the sun because it is one of the cheapest renewable energy sources. It is abundant and well distributed around the world. Solar fuels are scalable to cover global fuel demand.

![](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/cf83992731-1741344928/icon-leaf.svg)

**Eco-friendly**

The entire life cycle of solar fuels is based on environmentally-friendly principles. The fuels are produced with renewable energy and emit not only less CO2 but also less noxious gases than conventional fuels.

![](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/71841b5a4d-1741344928/icon-storage.svg)

**Storable**

Liquid fuels have a very high energy density. They can be transported and stored for a long time without compromising their quality. A winning combination of two non-negotiables for many fuel-consuming industries.

![](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/a87684959b-1741344928/icon-flag.svg)

**Independent**

Ideal conditions to produce solar fuels can be found on any continent. The independence from fossil resources results in greater autonomy. Shorter transportation routes are another key asset.

![](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/651d873b2c-1741344927/map.png)

**Circular**

Our solar fuels close the fuel carbon cycle. They only release as much CO2 into the atmosphere as was used for their production.

![An airplane being refueled.](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/b12fcf3be5-1741604581/plane-fueling-700x400-q100.jpg)
![A container ship on sea.](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/c3af52729b-1741604581/cargo-ship-700x400-q100.jpg)
![Several trucks on a parking lot.](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/a8be5f0641-1741604581/trucks-700x400-q100.jpg)
![Cars and trucks driving on a freeway.](https://synhelion.com/media/pages/our-products/84720989ac-1741604581/freeway-700x400-q100.jpg)

# Solar fuels for the transportation sector

Our focus is on defossilizing those transportation sectors that are difficult to electrify, such as aviation and shipping. In road transportation, electrification plays a key role in reducing CO2 emissions. However, studies show that there will be many remaining internal combustion engine vehicles around the world that will also need to be powered sustainably to meet net-zero emission targets by 2050. As a complementary solution to road electrification, Synhelion’s solar fuels can help achieve these targets.

[Contact](https://synhelion.com/contact) [Careers](https://synhelion.com/about/careers)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Solar fuels are synthetic fuels produced using solar energy through various methods like photochemical, photobiological, and thermochemical processes, allowing for sustainable energy storage.
Common types of solar fuels include hydrogen, ammonia, and liquid hydrocarbons like methanol, which can be produced from water and carbon dioxide using solar energy.
Solar fuels play a crucial role in the global energy transition, offering a sustainable alternative to fossil fuels while ensuring energy security and reducing dependency on finite resources.
Unlike fossil fuels, solar fuels are renewable, environmentally friendly, and can be produced locally, which reduces transportation emissions and enhances energy independence.
Solar fuels have significant potential to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, as their life cycle can be designed to emit equal or less CO2 than produced during their generation.
